text: The laparoscopic view reveals significant lump formation with adhesions. The omentum and cecum are visibly adhered to the lateral abdominal wall, indicating a challenging dissection environment.
The surgeon carefully detaches the omentum, revealing that the ileocecal junction is adhered to the lateral abdominal wall.

The surgeon medializes the cecum by detaching it from the lateral abdominal wall, allowing the cecum to be flipped medially. This maneuver facilitates locating the appendix and assessing its attachment, enabling an approach from the lateral side.
The surgeon approaches the appendix laterally, following the completion of the Cheetle's maneuver to medialize the cecum.
The surgeon approaches the appendix from the lateral side, lifting the cecum to expose the appendix for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the appendix after medializing the cecum and flipping it over, revealing the appendix's location for further dissection.
The surgeon carefully strips off the peritoneum to prevent serosal tears during the appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon has localized the appendix, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully removes adhesions around the appendix with precise and patient dissection.

The surgeon carefully separates the burst tip of the appendix, which is buried in the omentum, strand by strand. The omentum is known to bleed heavily, so the surgeon proceeds slowly to remove adhesions one by one.
The surgeon carefully examines the attachments to the appendix, with visible smoke and fogging occurring in the surgical field.
The surgeon has medialized the entire cecum, shifting it to expose the appendix and surrounding structures for further dissection.
The surgeon grasps the appendix with surgical instruments, preparing for further manipulation or dissection.
The terminal ileum is visibly adhered to the appendix near the ileocecal junction, showing a tight attachment between these structures.
The video shows the site of perforation where the terminal ileum adhered to the appendix, highlighting the area of tissue connection.
The omentum is visibly adhered to the appendix and surrounding structures, forming a large lump in the surgical field.
The surgeon strips the serosa from the appendix while carefully preserving the serosa of the adjacent ileum, demonstrating precise tissue manipulation.
The surgeon carefully strips the peritoneum from the lateral abdominal wall during desiolysis, ensuring not to damage the serosa of the cecum or ascending colon.
The surgeon visually inspects the base of the appendix, focusing on its anatomical position and surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects close to the appendix, minimizing manipulation of surrounding tissues to avoid unnecessary damage.
The surgical field shows significant bleeding around the appendix, with visible blood pooling in the area.
The laparoscopic view shows a surgical field with visible bleeding, estimated to be around twenty milliliters.
The surgeon performs a thorough wash of the surgical area before completing the appendectomy, ensuring the removal of any residual blood or debris.
The surgeon reassesses the anatomical structures involved in the dissection, ensuring all areas are clearly visible and properly evaluated.
The surgeon visually inspects the omentum, observing its appearance and position within the surgical field.
The surgeon visually inspects the cecum, assessing its condition and any involvement in the surgical field.
The surgeon visually inspects the ileum, examining its surface for any abnormalities or signs of damage.
The surgeon prepares to remove the appendix, which appears inflamed and potentially perforated, using an endo bag for specimen retrieval to prevent contamination.
